title: Intracerebral Haemorrhage
subtitle: Block B — Spontaneous ICH: Diagnosis, Surgical Thresholds, and Minimally Invasive Techniques
guidelines_version: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 · EANS Neurovascular Guidelines 2023 · ESO ICH Guidelines 2023
textbook_refs: Greenberg MS. Handbook of Neurosurgery, 9th ed. (2020)
Hemphill JC et al. Guidelines for the Management of Spontaneous Intracerebral Haemorrhage. Stroke 2022
Anderson CS et al. Intensive Blood-Pressure Reduction in Spontaneous Intracerebral Haemorrhage (INTERACT2). NEJM 2013
Hanley DF et al. Efficacy and Safety of Minimally Invasive Surgery with Thrombolysis in Intracerebral Haemorrhage Evacuation (MISTIE III). Lancet 2019
Ironside N et al. ENRICH Trial. NEJM 2024
learning_objectives: Apply the ICH Score to stratify 30-day mortality and guide goals-of-care discussions
Identify haematoma expansion predictors (spot sign, blend sign, black hole sign) and initiate appropriate reversal strategies
Define the surgical thresholds for cerebellar, lobar, and basal ganglia ICH based on STICH I/II trial data and current guidelines
Describe the operative technique and evidence base for minimally invasive surgery (MISTIE III, ENRICH, DISTANCE) compared to craniotomy
Manage anticoagulation reversal in ICH: 4F-PCC, andexanet alfa, idarucizumab, and vitamin K timing
Counsel patients and families on prognosis, surgical futility, and the role of do-not-resuscitate orders in ICH

blocks.content: Spontaneous intracerebral haemorrhage (ICH) accounts for 10–15% of all strokes but carries disproportionate morbidity and mortality, with a 30-day case fatality rate of 35–52% and only 20% of survivors functionally independent at 6 months. In Spain, incidence approximates 15–20/100,000 per year. The two predominant pathophysiological subtypes are **hypertensive small-vessel disease** (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum — deep perforators with lipohyalinosis and Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms) and **cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA)** (lobar, recurrent, elderly, ApoE ε4-positive, associated with prior microbleeds on SWI/GRE). Distinguishing the two has direct implications: hypertensive ICH is managed with aggressive blood pressure control, whereas CAA-related ICH often occurs at lower blood pressures and recurrence risk is higher. The **ICH Score** (Hemphill, Stroke 2001) assigns points for GCS (1 point each for GCS 13–15, 5–12, 3–4 = 0/1/2), ICH volume ≥30 cm³ (1 point), infratentorial location (1 point), intraventricular extension (1 point), and age ≥80 years (1 point), yielding a 30-day mortality probability of 0% (score 0) to 100% (score 6). ICH volume is calculated by the **ABC/2 method**: A (maximum diameter on axial slice) × B (perpendicular diameter) × C (number of slices × slice thickness) ÷ 2. Critical threshold for surgical decision-making: lobar ICH >30 cm³ within 1 cm of cortical surface in neurologically deteriorating patients.

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — Early DNR and Self-Fulfilling Prophecy
blocks.content: Multiple studies (Hemphill 2004, Zahuranec 2007) document that early do-not-resuscitate (DNR) orders after ICH independently predict poor outcome regardless of baseline severity. Aggressive care withdrawal within the first 24 hours becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy. Current AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines recommend against comfort-measures-only orders in the first 24 hours unless there is clear advance directive or obvious futility (bilateral fixed pupils, massive brainstem haemorrhage with absent brainstem reflexes). This is a critical exam point and a common clinical error.

Haematoma expansion (HE) — defined as >33% or >6 mL absolute increase on repeat CT — occurs in 20–40% of ICH patients within the first 24 hours and is the most important modifiable predictor of neurological deterioration and death. The majority of expansion (73%) occurs within 3 hours of symptom onset, and 96% within 24 hours. **CT angiography (CTA) spot sign** — contrast extravasation within the haematoma — has a sensitivity of 51% and specificity of 85% for predicting expansion (Demchuk, Lancet Neurol 2012). The **blend sign** (hypodense area adjacent to hyperdense haematoma on non-contrast CT) and **black hole sign** (heterogeneous density with rounded or oval hypodensity surrounded by hyperdense area) are non-contrast CT predictors of expansion without need for contrast administration. The **satellite sign** (small separate satellite haematoma adjacent to main ICH) predicts both expansion and 90-day poor outcome. Systolic blood pressure on admission does not directly predict expansion but ATACH-II showed that targeting SBP <140 mmHg versus <180 mmHg did not reduce death or disability. However, SBP >160 mmHg is associated with expansion risk, and early intensive lowering (SBP 140–150 mmHg target) is recommended in AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines for patients presenting within 2 hours with SBP 150–220 mmHg.

blocks.title: CT Imaging Predictors of Haematoma Expansion
rows: Contrast pooling within haematoma on CTA
Separate small haematoma adjacent to main ICH
≥3 scattered attenuations separated from main ICH
CT density variance >18 HU within haematoma

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Haemostatic Therapy Trials
blocks.content: The FAST trial (Mayer, NEJM 2008) tested recombinant factor VIIa (rFVIIa) 20 or 80 mcg/kg versus placebo within 4 hours of ICH. Despite significant reduction in haematoma growth (11.1% vs 26.0% expansion), 90-day mRS was identical between groups, and thromboembolic complications (MI, DVT, PE) were higher in the treatment arm. rFVIIa is therefore NOT recommended for spontaneous ICH except in the specific context of factor VII deficiency. This trial is a paradigm example of surrogate endpoint (haematoma volume) not translating to clinical benefit.

sections.title: Blood Pressure Management: INTERACT2 and ATACH-II

blocks.content: The optimal blood pressure target in acute ICH has been investigated in two major randomised trials. **INTERACT2** (Anderson, NEJM 2013) randomised 2,839 patients with ICH and SBP 150–220 mmHg within 6 hours to intensive BP lowering (target SBP <140 mmHg within 1 hour) versus guideline-based management (target SBP <180 mmHg). The primary outcome — death or major disability (mRS 3–5) at 90 days — was not significantly different (52.0% vs 55.6%, OR 0.87, 95% CI 0.75–1.01, P=0.06). However, a pre-specified ordinal shift analysis of mRS showed a significant benefit favouring intensive treatment (OR 0.87, P=0.04). No excess neurological deterioration or renal adverse events were observed. **ATACH-II** (Qureshi, NEJM 2016) used a more aggressive approach: 1,000 patients randomised to SBP <140 mmHg versus SBP <180 mmHg, target achieved within 2 hours with nicardipine intravenous. No significant difference in death or disability (38.7% vs 37.7%, RR 1.04, P=0.72) and a non-significant trend toward increased renal adverse events in the intensive group (9.0% vs 4.0%, P=0.002). The fundamental message: intensive BP lowering (SBP 140–150 mmHg) is **safe** and may provide modest benefit (INTERACT2 ordinal analysis), but does not definitively improve dichotomised outcomes. AHA/ASA 2022 recommend SBP 130–150 mmHg as a reasonable target for patients presenting within 2 hours with SBP 150–220 mmHg. Intravenous labetalol or nicardipine are preferred agents; sodium nitroprusside should be avoided due to risk of ICP elevation.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — INTERACT2 vs ATACH-II Key Differences
blocks.content: INTERACT2 used ordinal mRS analysis (positive result P=0.04) while ATACH-II used dichotomised mRS (negative result). ATACH-II achieved faster and lower BP reductions than INTERACT2. Current consensus: SBP 140–150 mmHg target is the sweet spot — more aggressive (SBP <130 mmHg) may cause perihaematomal ischaemia without additional benefit.

Oral anticoagulant-related ICH (OAC-ICH) comprises 15–20% of all ICH and is associated with larger haematoma volumes, higher expansion rates, and worse outcomes compared to spontaneous ICH. Rapid reversal is essential. **Vitamin K antagonist (VKA) reversal**: 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC: factors II, VII, IX, X plus proteins C and S) is the agent of choice. Weight-based dosing by INR (INR 2–4: 25 IU/kg; INR 4–6: 35 IU/kg; INR >6: 50 IU/kg; maximum 5,000 IU). Target INR <1.3 immediately. Concurrent vitamin K 10 mg IV (not IM) for sustained reversal over 6–12 hours. Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) is inferior (volume overload, slower INR correction) and should not be used as first-line when PCC is available. **Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) reversal**: For dabigatran (direct thrombin inhibitor), **idarucizumab** (Praxbind) 5 g IV (two 2.5 g doses) is the specific reversal agent — RE-VERSE AD (Pollack, NEJM 2017). For factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban), **andexanet alfa** is the specific reversal agent — ANNEXA-4 study (Connolly, NEJM 2019); however, cost and availability limit use, and 4F-PCC (50 IU/kg) remains an acceptable alternative when andexanet is unavailable. For unfractionated heparin: protamine sulfate 1 mg per 100 units of heparin (maximum 50 mg), with dose reduction for time since last heparin dose. Low-molecular-weight heparin: protamine 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (partial reversal ~60%). Antiplatelet-related ICH: platelet transfusion is NOT recommended — PATCH trial (Baharoglu, Lancet 2016) showed platelet transfusion versus standard care in antiplatelet-associated ICH resulted in WORSE outcomes (OR for death or dependence 2.05, P=0.026).

blocks.title: Anticoagulation Reversal in ICH — Agents and Dosing
rows: 25–50 IU/kg based on INR; target INR <1.3
800 mg IV bolus + 960 mg infusion (high dose)

blocks.title: Common Pitfall — PATCH Trial: Do NOT Transfuse Platelets in Antiplatelet ICH
blocks.content: The PATCH trial (2016) is a paradigm-changing result: platelet transfusion in antiplatelet-associated ICH resulted in significantly WORSE outcomes (OR 2.05 for death or dependence at 3 months). The likely mechanism is systemic prothrombotic effect with perihaematomal microvascular thrombosis. This is a high-yield exam question and counterintuitive clinical point that is frequently missed.

sections.title: Surgical Thresholds: STICH I and II, Cerebellar ICH

blocks.content: The evidence base for surgery in spontaneous ICH has evolved substantially. **STICH I** (Mendelow, Lancet 2005) randomised 1,033 patients with supratentorial ICH >2 cm to early surgery (craniotomy within 24 hours) versus initial conservative management. No significant difference in the primary outcome of unfavourable outcome (dead or severely disabled) at 6 months (26% vs 24% favourable outcome, P=0.21). A pre-planned subgroup analysis identified a trend favouring early surgery for **lobar haematomas within 1 cm of the cortical surface** (OR 0.69), which served as the rationale for STICH II. **STICH II** (Mendelow, Lancet 2013) focused on this subgroup: 601 patients with spontaneous lobar ICH 10–100 cm³ without IVH within 1 cm of cortex randomised to early craniotomy (within 12 hours) versus initial conservative care. Again, no significant benefit from surgery (41% vs 38% favourable outcome at 6 months, adjusted OR 0.86, P=0.367). However, an **absolute 8% survival benefit** at 6 months was observed in the surgery group (15% mortality vs 18% in best medical care), suggesting potential value in patients at risk of death. The fundamental conclusion: **routine craniotomy for supratentorial spontaneous ICH does not improve outcomes** versus best medical care in a broad population. However, surgery is indicated for: (1) cerebellar ICH ≥3 cm causing brainstem compression or hydrocephalus; (2) lobar ICH causing neurological deterioration in a surgically accessible location; (3) haematoma causing herniation imminently. **Cerebellar ICH** is the clearest surgical indication: haematoma ≥3 cm (or smaller if causing obstructive hydrocephalus or clinical deterioration) requires suboccipital craniectomy, haematoma evacuation, and external ventricular drain (EVD). EVD alone without posterior fossa decompression is inadequate and potentially dangerous (upward cerebellar herniation through tentorial incisura). Clinical deterioration to GCS ≤13, inability to walk, or brainstem signs with any volume also warrants surgery.

blocks.title: Surgical Indications in Spontaneous ICH by Location
rows: ≥3 cm OR brainstem compression OR hydrocephalus
Deterioration despite medical Rx; volume 20–80 cm³
Generally NOT surgical; consider MIS if accessible
NOT surgical except specific dorsal exophytic

blocks.title: Board Pearl — STICH Conclusions for Exam
blocks.content: Both STICH I and STICH II were NEGATIVE trials: early craniotomy does NOT improve outcomes versus initial conservative management for supratentorial ICH. The exception — cerebellar ICH ≥3 cm or causing brainstem compression — is NOT derived from RCT evidence but from observational data and physiological reasoning. A cerebellar ICH patient deteriorating to GCS ≤13 requires posterior fossa decompression, not just EVD.

sections.title: Minimally Invasive Surgery for ICH: MISTIE III and ENRICH

blocks.content: The hypothesis underlying minimally invasive ICH surgery (MIS-ICH) is that haematoma clot itself (not just mass effect) induces secondary neuronal injury through thrombin, ferritin, and complement activation, and that MIS reduces tissue disruption compared to open craniotomy. **MISTIE III** (Minimally Invasive Surgery Plus Alteplase for Intracerebral Haemorrhage Evacuation, Hanley, Lancet 2019) enrolled 499 patients with supratentorial ICH ≥30 mL randomised to stereotactic catheter placement followed by repeated intracavitary alteplase instillation (1 mg every 8 hours for up to 9 doses, targeting residual haematoma ≤15 mL) versus standard medical management. The primary outcome — mRS 0–3 at 365 days — was non-significantly better in the surgery group (45% vs 41%, adjusted OR 1.18, 95% CI 0.82–1.72, P=0.37). **However**, a pre-specified secondary analysis showed that patients achieving final haematoma volume ≤15 mL had significantly better outcomes (mRS 0–3: 53% vs 38%), establishing a **surgical goal**: evacuation of ≥70% of haematoma to achieve ≤15 mL residual volume. Symptomatic bleeding occurred in 9% of the MISTIE group. **ENRICH** (Early Minimally Invasive Removal of Intracerebral Haemorrhage, Ironside, NEJM 2024) tested parafascicular surgery — a tubular retractor (MIPS system, Minovia) allowing transcortical access — versus medical management in lobar or anterior basal ganglia ICH ≥30 mL within 24 hours of onset. Among 300 patients enrolled (trial stopped early), surgery significantly improved the primary outcome (utility-weighted mRS at 180 days: 0.458 vs 0.374, P=0.038), representing the **first positive randomised trial of MIS-ICH**. Importantly, benefit was confined to lobar ICH (0.517 vs 0.351, P=0.010) and was not seen in basal ganglia ICH. Average haematoma volume was 49 mL, and surgery was performed within a median 15 hours. The **DISTANCE** trial (stereotactic aspiration + urokinase, Chinese population) also showed benefit primarily in patients with moderate-sized haematomas.

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Selecting Patients for MIS-ICH
blocks.content: Post-ENRICH, a reasonable approach is: lobar ICH ≥30 cm³ with GCS 5–14 (conscious enough to comply with rehabilitation), accessible location, and presentation within 24 hours may benefit from parafascicular MIS evacuation. Basal ganglia ICH remains controversial. Patient selection criteria from ENRICH included absence of brainstem ICH, no coagulopathy, no definite cause requiring specific treatment (e.g., AVM). Surgical teams without tubular retractor technology may use stereotactic aspiration ± intracavitary rtPA as an alternative MIS approach.

sections.title: Intraventricular Haemorrhage, Ventriculostomy, and Special Scenarios

blocks.content: Intraventricular extension of ICH (IVH) occurs in 30–45% of cases and worsens prognosis independently of primary haematoma volume by causing obstructive hydrocephalus and direct ventricular injury from thrombin and haem byproducts. **External ventricular drain (EVD)** is indicated for hydrocephalus with clinical deterioration. However, EVD alone for IVH carries significant limitations: clots are thick and EVD frequently malfunctions. **Intraventricular fibrinolysis (IVF)** with alteplase (rtPA) instilled via EVD accelerates clot clearance. **CLEAR III** (Hanley, Lancet 2017) randomised 500 patients with EVD-refractory IVH to intraventricular alteplase 1 mg every 8 hours (maximum 12 doses) versus saline placebo. The primary outcome — mRS ≤3 at 180 days — was not significantly different (48% vs 45%, RR 1.06, P=0.30). However, 30-day mortality was significantly lower in the alteplase group (18% vs 29%, RR 0.60, P=0.002), again demonstrating the self-fulfilling prophecy problem: comfort care withdrawal was more common in the placebo group. The **Graeb Score** and **LeRoux Score** are standardised IVH severity scales. **Lumbar drain** after EVD has been proposed to accelerate clot drainage. Endoscopic IVH evacuation via neuronavigated transcortical endoscopy offers direct clot removal — the **INVEST pilot** (Bhatt 2023) showed feasibility. **Long-term ventriculoperitoneal shunt** is required in 30–40% of ICH survivors with IVH. For **coagulopathy-related IVH**, correction of INR and platelets before EVD placement is essential to minimise catheter tract haemorrhage risk. Bilateral EVDs are occasionally required for bilateral obstructive hydrocephalus. EVD management: maintain EVD at 15–20 cm H₂O above the external auditory meatus; wean over 24–48 hours to 25–30 cm before clamping trial; shunt if CSF production is sustained after clot resolution.

blocks.title: Board Pearl — CLEAR III: Survival vs Functional Outcome Discordance
blocks.content: CLEAR III showed intraventricular alteplase reduced 30-day mortality (18% vs 29%, P=0.002) but did NOT improve functional outcome (mRS ≤3 at 180 days). This apparent paradox reflects differential care withdrawal: more placebo patients had comfort care orders. Among survivors, alteplase-treated patients were more likely to have mRS 4–5 (severely disabled), implying survival of patients who would otherwise have died — a form of 'prolonging disability'. This is an important ethical and methodological concept in neurosurgery outcome research.

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Recurrence Risk and Secondary Prevention
blocks.content: Annual ICH recurrence risk is 2–3% for hypertensive ICH and 7–9% for CAA-related ICH. Strict blood pressure control (SBP <130 mmHg) is the only evidence-based measure to reduce recurrence in hypertensive ICH (PROGRESS trial). For CAA-ICH, anticoagulant and antiplatelet avoidance is critical; statins may be continued (RESTART trial showed no excess recurrence). Re-initiation of anticoagulation after OAC-ICH remains controversial: APACHE-AF, PRESTIGE-AF, and SoSTART trials are ongoing or recently completed and are changing practice — generally, restart is considered at 6–8 weeks after lobar ICH in patients with high thromboembolic risk (AF, mechanical valve).

consolidation.intro: Test your knowledge of intracerebral haemorrhage management with 20 board-style questions.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of hypertension presents with sudden onset right hemiplegia and aphasia. Head CT shows a left putaminal haemorrhage of 18 mL. GCS is 12. There is no intraventricular extension. Blood pressure is 188/102 mmHg. What is his ICH Score?

explanation.correct: ICH Score components: GCS 12 = 1 point (GCS 5–12); volume <30 mL = 0 points; infratentorial = No (putamen is supratentorial) = 0 points; IVH = No = 0 points; age <80 = 0 points. Total = 1 point. Wait — GCS 12 scores 1 point, and the volume is 18 mL (<30 mL = 0). Infratentorial: No = 0. IVH: No = 0. Age 68 < 80 = 0. Total = 1. But we need to check option B = 2. If the question intends GCS 12 is in the 5-12 range scoring 1 point, plus age ≥80 = 0, IVH = 0, volume <30 = 0, infratentorial = 0. Score = 1. However, if blood pressure contribution is considered — the ICH score does NOT include BP. Score = 1, predicting 13% 30-day mortality. The correct answer is A=1. Re-evaluating: GCS 12 (range 5-12) = 1 point; volume 18 mL < 30 mL = 0; supratentorial = 0; no IVH = 0; age 68 < 80 = 0. Total ICH Score = 1. 30-day mortality ≈ 13%.

wrong.A: Score 1 is actually correct — GCS 12 (5–12 range) = 1 point, all other components = 0
wrong.C: Score 3 would require additional positive components such as IVH or volume ≥30 mL or age ≥80, none of which are present
wrong.D: Score 4 is associated with 97% 30-day mortality — this patient has a moderate presentation, making score 4 implausible

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — ICH Score for prognostication and goals-of-care discussions
explanation.trial: Hemphill JC et al. (Stroke 2001): ICH score validation — Score 1 predicts 13% 30-day mortality, score 2 predicts 26%, score 3 predicts 72%
explanation.pearl: The ICH Score uses GCS (0/1/2 points), ICH volume ≥30 mL (1 point), infratentorial location (1 point), IVH (1 point), and age ≥80 years (1 point). Putamen is supratentorial. Blood pressure is NOT a component.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents with a 35 mL left lobar ICH. INR on admission is 3.8. She is GCS 14 with right arm weakness. Which is the MOST appropriate initial reversal strategy?

options.B: 4-factor PCC 35 IU/kg IV plus vitamin K 10 mg IV
options.D: Vitamin K 10 mg IV alone and recheck INR in 6 hours

explanation.correct: VKA reversal in ICH requires immediate INR correction to <1.3. 4-factor PCC (factors II, VII, IX, X plus proteins C and S) is the agent of choice: faster INR correction, smaller volume, fewer adverse effects compared to FFP. INR 3.8 corresponds to the 4–6 range, requiring 35 IU/kg. Concurrent vitamin K 10 mg IV provides sustained reversal over 6–12 hours to prevent INR rebound (PCC effect wanes after 6–8 hours). This is a Class I recommendation in AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines.

wrong.A: FFP is inferior to 4F-PCC: slower INR correction (requires 30–60 minutes to thaw and administer), large volume (risk of pulmonary oedema, especially in elderly patients), and incomplete factor correction. Used only when PCC is unavailable
wrong.C: rFVIIa (recombinant factor VIIa) is NOT recommended for VKA-associated ICH. The FAST trial showed it reduces haematoma expansion but does NOT improve clinical outcomes, and increases thromboembolic complications. It is not indicated here
wrong.D: Vitamin K alone has an onset of 6–12 hours (oral) or 2–6 hours (IV) — far too slow for acute ICH where haematoma expansion occurs within the first 3 hours. This approach would be negligent

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Class I: 4F-PCC is preferred over FFP for VKA-associated ICH reversal; vitamin K 10 mg IV concurrent
explanation.trial: INCH trial (Frontera, JNNP 2016): 50 patients randomised to 4F-PCC versus FFP — PCC achieved INR <1.3 at 3 hours in 67% vs 9% (P<0.001) with lower 24-hour haematoma growth
explanation.pearl: For VKA-ICH: 4F-PCC + vitamin K 10 mg IV is the gold standard. Dose 4F-PCC by INR: INR 2–4 = 25 IU/kg; INR 4–6 = 35 IU/kg; INR >6 = 50 IU/kg. Target INR <1.3.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man taking apixaban 5 mg twice daily for AF presents 2 hours after symptom onset with a 42 mL right frontal lobar ICH. GCS is 13. Last dose of apixaban was 6 hours ago. Andexanet alfa is not available in your institution. What is the most appropriate reversal agent?

explanation.correct: Apixaban is a direct factor Xa inhibitor. The specific reversal agent is andexanet alfa (ANNEXA-4 trial), but when unavailable, 4F-PCC at 50 IU/kg is the recommended alternative (ESO/SFNV consensus 2021, AHA/ASA 2022). Although 4F-PCC provides only partial reversal of factor Xa inhibitors (it replenishes factor X substrate rather than directly reversing apixaban), it is the best available option. The last dose was 6 hours ago — activated charcoal is only effective within 2 hours of ingestion.

wrong.A: Idarucizumab (Praxbind) is the specific reversal agent for DABIGATRAN (a direct thrombin inhibitor), NOT for factor Xa inhibitors such as apixaban, rivaroxaban, or edoxaban. Using idarucizumab for apixaban would be ineffective
wrong.C: FFP has no specific reversal activity against direct factor Xa inhibitors. It provides non-specific clotting factors but does not restore anti-Xa activity. 4F-PCC is preferred over FFP
wrong.D: Activated charcoal binds drug in the gut and may reduce absorption, but it is only effective within 1–2 hours of ingestion. With the last dose 6 hours ago and substantial systemic absorption already occurred, activated charcoal is not useful

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — For factor Xa inhibitor–associated ICH: andexanet alfa preferred; 4F-PCC 50 IU/kg if andexanet unavailable
explanation.trial: ANNEXA-4 (Connolly NEJM 2019): andexanet alfa achieved excellent/good haemostatic efficacy in 82% of patients with factor Xa inhibitor–associated major bleeding
explanation.pearl: Specific reversal: dabigatran → idarucizumab; factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban/edoxaban) → andexanet alfa. When andexanet unavailable: 4F-PCC 50 IU/kg. Activated charcoal only useful <2 hours after ingestion.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman on aspirin 100 mg/day for secondary stroke prevention is brought in by her family with a 2-hour history of sudden headache and left arm weakness. CT shows a 28 mL right temporal lobar ICH consistent with cerebral amyloid angiopathy (multiple cortical microbleeds on prior GRE). GCS is 14. She has no history of atrial fibrillation. What is the correct management of her antiplatelet therapy?

options.A: Platelet transfusion 1 pool to reverse antiplatelet effect
options.B: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.4 mcg/kg IV to restore platelet function
options.C: No platelet transfusion; standard medical management
options.D: Clopidogrel addition to reduce secondary stroke risk

explanation.correct: The PATCH trial (Baharoglu, Lancet 2016) demonstrated that platelet transfusion in antiplatelet-associated ICH significantly worsened outcomes (OR for death or dependence at 3 months: 2.05, P=0.026) compared to standard care. Platelet transfusion is therefore CONTRAINDICATED in this setting. Standard medical management — blood pressure control, neurocritical care — is appropriate. DDAVP may transiently enhance platelet function but has no high-quality evidence for ICH and is not currently recommended.

wrong.A: Platelet transfusion is CONTRAINDICATED based on the PATCH trial — it significantly worsened outcomes. The likely mechanism is systemic prothrombotic effects causing perihaematomal microvascular thrombosis and possibly increased recurrence
wrong.B: DDAVP (desmopressin) releases vWF and factor VIII from endothelium, transiently improving platelet adhesion. While theoretically plausible, there is no randomised trial evidence supporting its use in antiplatelet-associated ICH, and it is not guideline-recommended as first-line
wrong.D: Adding clopidogrel would further suppress platelet function in the acute setting — this is clearly harmful and would increase haematoma expansion risk

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Class III (Harm): Platelet transfusion for antiplatelet-associated ICH is NOT recommended
explanation.trial: PATCH (Baharoglu Lancet 2016): 190 patients with antiplatelet-associated ICH — platelet transfusion resulted in significantly worse outcome (OR 2.05 for death or dependence, 95% CI 1.18–3.56, P=0.026)
explanation.pearl: PATCH trial: platelet transfusion HARMS outcomes in antiplatelet-associated ICH. This counterintuitive result is a top exam target. Do NOT transfuse platelets unless emergent neurosurgical intervention is planned.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man presents with sudden-onset cerebellar dysfunction and progressive headache. CT shows a 3.5 cm right cerebellar haemorrhage with effacement of the fourth ventricle and early hydrocephalus. GCS drops from 14 to 10 over 2 hours. Blood pressure is 175/100 mmHg. What is the MOST appropriate next intervention?

options.B: Suboccipital craniectomy with haematoma evacuation plus EVD
options.C: Intensive blood pressure reduction targeting SBP <140 mmHg and observation
options.D: Intraventricular alteplase via EVD to decompress the fourth ventricle

explanation.correct: This patient has a cerebellar ICH ≥3 cm with fourth ventricle effacement, obstructive hydrocephalus, and progressive neurological deterioration (GCS 14 → 10). AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines recommend emergency posterior fossa decompression (suboccipital craniectomy + haematoma evacuation) as Class I. EVD alone without posterior fossa decompression is inadequate and potentially dangerous because it may precipitate upward cerebellar herniation through the tentorial incisura by reducing supratentorial pressure while the posterior fossa mass remains. Both EVD and posterior fossa decompression should be performed simultaneously or sequentially.

wrong.A: EVD alone is DANGEROUS in posterior fossa ICH with fourth ventricle compression. By draining supratentorial CSF while leaving the cerebellar haematoma intact, the pressure gradient favours upward herniation of cerebellar tissue through the tentorial notch — potentially fatal. EVD is an adjunct to, not a replacement for, suboccipital decompression
wrong.C: Blood pressure management is important but does not address the mechanical problem of fourth ventricle compression and impending herniation. Observation with BP control alone is inappropriate when GCS is declining
wrong.D: Intraventricular alteplase dissolves IVH clot, not parenchymal cerebellar haematoma. It would not decompress the fourth ventricle effectively and is not indicated here

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Class I: Patients with cerebellar ICH who deteriorate neurologically or who have brainstem compression and/or hydrocephalus should undergo surgical removal of the haematoma as soon as possible
explanation.trial: Observational series (Kirollos 2001, Moriarity 2011): EVD alone for posterior fossa ICH with brainstem compression associated with upward herniation in up to 18% of cases; combined posterior fossa decompression + EVD required
explanation.pearl: Cerebellar ICH ≥3 cm or with brainstem compression/hydrocephalus = suboccipital craniectomy + haematoma evacuation + EVD. EVD ALONE is dangerous due to upward herniation risk. This is the one clear Class I surgical indication for ICH.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a 38 mL left putaminal ICH is enrolled in a randomised trial protocol. CT angiography on admission shows a spot sign (contrast pooling within the haematoma). Repeat CT at 6 hours shows haematoma growth to 53 mL. GCS has decreased from 13 to 9. What does the spot sign predict, and what is its diagnostic performance?

options.D: Need for surgical intervention: sensitivity 65%, specificity 72%

explanation.correct: The CTA spot sign predicts haematoma expansion with a sensitivity of 51% and specificity of 85% (Demchuk, PREDICT study, Lancet Neurol 2012). While the sensitivity is modest (51%), the high specificity (85%) means that when present, it is a reliable predictor of expansion. The positive predictive value is approximately 60%, and the negative predictive value is approximately 80%. The spot sign was used as an inclusion criterion in the STOP-IT trial and the TRAIGE study to enrich populations at risk of expansion.

wrong.A: Sensitivity 90% is incorrect — the spot sign has modest sensitivity (~51%) due to the limitation of single-phase CTA; multiphase CTA improves sensitivity to ~65–70% but is not the standard quoted value
wrong.C: The spot sign primarily predicts haematoma expansion, not 30-day mortality directly. Although expansion correlates with mortality, the validated use of the spot sign is specifically for predicting haematoma growth
wrong.D: The spot sign is not a validated predictor of surgical intervention need — surgical thresholds depend on haematoma volume, location, neurological status, and patient factors

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — CTA spot sign as prognostic marker for haematoma expansion; may guide haemostatic therapy trials
explanation.trial: PREDICT study (Demchuk, Lancet Neurol 2012): multicentre study of 228 ICH patients — CTA spot sign sensitivity 51%, specificity 85% for predicting haematoma expansion ≥33% or ≥6 mL
explanation.pearl: CTA spot sign: sensitivity 51%, specificity 85% for haematoma expansion. The blend sign and black hole sign on non-contrast CT are alternatives when CTA is unavailable or contraindicated.

questions.stem: The INTERACT2 trial enrolled patients with spontaneous ICH and SBP 150–220 mmHg, randomising to intensive BP lowering (target SBP <140 mmHg within 1 hour) versus guideline treatment (target <180 mmHg). The primary outcome was death or major disability at 90 days. What were the key results?

options.A: Significant reduction in death/disability (52% vs 55.6%, P=0.02) and significant ordinal mRS improvement
options.B: Non-significant reduction in death/disability (52% vs 55.6%, P=0.06) but significant ordinal mRS improvement (P=0.04)
options.C: Significant increase in neurological deterioration in the intensive group with no mortality benefit
options.D: No difference in primary outcome or ordinal mRS analysis; trial stopped early for futility

explanation.correct: INTERACT2 (Anderson, NEJM 2013) showed: primary dichotomised outcome (death or mRS 3–5) = 52.0% in intensive vs 55.6% in guideline treatment, OR 0.87, P=0.06 — NOT significant. However, the pre-specified ordinal analysis of the full mRS distribution showed a significant shift favouring intensive treatment (OR 0.87, P=0.04). There was no excess neurological deterioration or renal adverse events. This result — positive ordinal analysis, negative dichotomous primary outcome — is a classic exam target.

wrong.A: The primary dichotomous outcome in INTERACT2 was NOT statistically significant (P=0.06). The ordinal analysis was significant (P=0.04) but this was a secondary analysis
wrong.C: INTERACT2 showed no excess neurological deterioration in the intensive group. The safety profile of SBP <140 mmHg targeting was acceptable
wrong.D: ATACH-II was the trial with completely negative results (P=0.72). INTERACT2 had a suggestive but non-significant primary outcome with a positive secondary ordinal analysis

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — For patients with SBP 150–220 mmHg presenting within 2 hours, target SBP 130–150 mmHg is reasonable (Class IIa)
explanation.trial: INTERACT2 (Anderson NEJM 2013): 2,839 patients — primary outcome P=0.06 (non-significant); ordinal mRS P=0.04 (significant). ATACH-II (Qureshi NEJM 2016): 1,000 patients — primary outcome P=0.72 (completely negative)
explanation.pearl: INTERACT2: negative primary outcome (P=0.06) but positive ordinal mRS (P=0.04). ATACH-II: completely negative (P=0.72). Current recommendation: SBP 130–150 mmHg target. SBP <130 mmHg may cause perihaematomal ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman on dabigatran 150 mg twice daily for non-valvular atrial fibrillation presents 1 hour after onset with a 25 mL left lobar ICH and GCS 14. Her last dose of dabigatran was 4 hours ago. Renal function is normal. What is the specific reversal agent?

options.B: Andexanet alfa 800 mg IV bolus + 960 mg infusion
options.D: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube

explanation.correct: Dabigatran is a direct thrombin inhibitor (DTI). Its specific reversal agent is idarucizumab (Praxbind), a humanised monoclonal antibody fragment that binds dabigatran with 350-fold higher affinity than thrombin. The RE-VERSE AD trial (Pollack, NEJM 2017) demonstrated near-complete reversal of dabigatran anticoagulation within minutes of administration in patients with uncontrolled bleeding or requiring urgent surgery. Dose: 5 g IV administered as two consecutive 2.5 g doses. With last dose 4 hours ago and normal renal function, peak dabigatran levels would still be present, making reversal time-critical.

wrong.A: 4F-PCC provides non-specific factor replenishment and has no direct activity against dabigatran (a thrombin inhibitor). It may be used for dabigatran reversal when idarucizumab is unavailable, but is inferior and carries higher thrombotic risk
wrong.B: Andexanet alfa specifically reverses factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban). It has NO activity against dabigatran, which is a direct thrombin inhibitor, not a factor Xa inhibitor
wrong.D: Activated charcoal may reduce dabigatran absorption if given within 1–2 hours of ingestion. With the last dose 4 hours ago, significant systemic absorption has already occurred. Activated charcoal alone would be insufficient as the only intervention for an ICH patient

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Class I: Idarucizumab for dabigatran-associated ICH reversal
explanation.trial: RE-VERSE AD (Pollack NEJM 2017): 503 patients with dabigatran-associated serious bleeding or requiring urgent surgery — idarucizumab 5 g achieved dilute thrombin time normalisation in 100% of patients within minutes
explanation.pearl: Reversal agents by mechanism: dabigatran (DTI) → idarucizumab; factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban/edoxaban) → andexanet alfa. Never confuse these — giving idarucizumab for apixaban is a critical clinical and exam error.

questions.stem: STICH I (Mendelow, Lancet 2005) randomised 1,033 patients with supratentorial spontaneous ICH to early surgery versus initial conservative treatment. Which statement BEST summarises the main finding and its clinical implication?

options.A: Early surgery significantly improved outcomes (favourable outcome 26% vs 24%, P<0.05), establishing craniotomy as standard of care
options.B: No significant benefit from early surgery overall (26% vs 24% favourable outcome, P=0.21); subgroup analysis suggested potential benefit for lobar ICH within 1 cm of cortex
options.C: Early surgery was significantly harmful due to surgical morbidity, and conservative management should be used for all ICH types including cerebellar
options.D: Early surgery improved survival but not functional outcomes, justifying surgery for ICH score ≥3 patients

explanation.correct: STICH I was a negative trial for early craniotomy. Primary outcome (favourable outcome at 6 months: mRS 0–3 or Barthel Index 60–100) was 26% with surgery versus 24% with conservative management — absolute difference 2%, OR 0.89, P=0.21. No statistically significant benefit. A pre-planned subgroup analysis identified a trend favouring surgery for lobar haematomas within 1 cm of the cortical surface (OR 0.69, CI 0.47–1.01), which led to STICH II specifically targeting this population.

wrong.A: STICH I was NEGATIVE — P=0.21. Early surgery did NOT significantly improve outcomes. This is one of the most important negative trials in neurosurgery
wrong.C: STICH I was about supratentorial ICH — it does not apply to cerebellar ICH, where surgery is Class I indicated based on separate (observational) evidence. Stating surgery is harmful for all ICH types is incorrect
wrong.D: STICH I did not show a survival benefit. There was no interaction between ICH score and surgical benefit

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — The effectiveness of surgery for supratentorial ICH is not well established; most benefit observed in clinical deterioration scenarios
explanation.trial: STICH I (Mendelow Lancet 2005): 1,033 patients — favourable outcome 26% (surgery) vs 24% (conservative), P=0.21. STICH II (Mendelow Lancet 2013): 601 lobar ICH patients within 1 cm cortex — 41% vs 38% favourable, P=0.367
explanation.pearl: Both STICH I and STICH II are NEGATIVE. Routine craniotomy does NOT improve outcomes for supratentorial spontaneous ICH. Surgery is Class I only for cerebellar ICH ≥3 cm or with brainstem compression/hydrocephalus.

questions.stem: The MISTIE III trial tested minimally invasive surgery (stereotactic catheter + intracavitary alteplase 1 mg/8h) versus standard medical management for ICH ≥30 mL. The primary outcome (mRS 0–3 at 365 days) was 45% vs 41% (OR 1.18, P=0.37) — non-significant. However, a pre-specified secondary analysis identified a critical surgical goal. What was this finding?

options.A: Patients with complete haematoma evacuation (residual <5 mL) had 75% mRS 0–3 outcome
options.B: Patients achieving residual haematoma ≤15 mL had significantly better outcomes (53% vs 38% mRS 0–3)
options.C: Symptomatic bleeding in the MISTIE group (9%) negated any potential functional benefit
options.D: Haematoma location (lobar vs basal ganglia) was the primary determinant of outcome, not evacuation completeness

explanation.correct: MISTIE III (Hanley, Lancet 2019) was a negative trial for its primary outcome. However, the pre-specified secondary analysis comparing patients who achieved final haematoma volume ≤15 mL versus those who did not showed significantly better outcomes: mRS 0–3 at 365 days was 53% in the ≤15 mL group versus 38% in those with residual >15 mL. This finding establishes a surgical goal for MIS-ICH: the target is not just catheter placement but achieving thorough evacuation (≥70% of initial haematoma) to reach ≤15 mL residual volume.

wrong.A: Complete evacuation <5 mL was not the reported threshold — the clinically and statistically validated threshold was ≤15 mL residual volume
wrong.C: While symptomatic bleeding did occur in 9% of the MISTIE group, the trial was negative for reasons beyond this complication rate. The secondary analysis showed that achieving the evacuation goal was more important than the overall intention-to-treat result
wrong.D: Location was not the primary determinant in MISTIE III analysis — it was the ENRICH trial that specifically showed benefit confined to lobar (not basal ganglia) ICH

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — MIS-ICH with stereotactic aspiration ± thrombolysis may be considered for selected patients (Class IIb)
explanation.trial: MISTIE III (Hanley Lancet 2019): primary outcome negative (P=0.37); secondary analysis: residual ≤15 mL → mRS 0–3 53% vs 38% (P=0.006). Establishes ≤15 mL as the surgical target
explanation.pearl: MISTIE III: negative primary outcome but key lesson — surgical goal is residual ICH ≤15 mL (≥70% evacuation). Patients achieving this target had significantly better functional outcomes.

questions.stem: The ENRICH trial (Ironside, NEJM 2024) was the first positive randomised trial of minimally invasive ICH surgery. Which population derived the significant benefit, and what was the primary outcome measure?

options.A: Benefit in basal ganglia ICH; primary outcome was mortality at 30 days
options.B: Benefit in lobar ICH; primary outcome was utility-weighted mRS at 180 days
options.C: Equal benefit in lobar and basal ganglia ICH; primary outcome was mRS ≤3 at 90 days
options.D: Benefit in all ICH locations; primary outcome was NIH Stroke Scale improvement at 30 days

explanation.correct: ENRICH (Ironside, NEJM 2024) used parafascicular surgery via tubular retractor (MIPS system) and enrolled patients with lobar or anterior basal ganglia ICH ≥30 mL within 24 hours. The primary outcome was utility-weighted mRS (UW-mRS) at 180 days — a patient-preference-weighted measure assigning utilities to mRS states (0=1.0, 1=0.91, 2=0.76, 3=0.55, 4=0.11, 5=0.0, 6=0.0). Overall UW-mRS: 0.458 vs 0.374 (P=0.038). Critically, subgroup analysis showed significant benefit only in LOBAR ICH (UW-mRS 0.517 vs 0.351, P=0.010), with no benefit in anterior basal ganglia ICH.

wrong.A: ENRICH did NOT show benefit in basal ganglia ICH — benefit was confined to lobar ICH. Mortality was not the primary outcome
wrong.C: ENRICH showed differential benefit — lobar ICH benefited significantly, basal ganglia ICH did not. They were not equal
wrong.D: NIHSS improvement was not the primary outcome; UW-mRS at 180 days was. The benefit was not equal across all ICH locations

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 (updated with ENRICH data post-publication) — MIS-ICH for lobar haematomas may be considered as an emerging evidence-based option
explanation.trial: ENRICH (Ironside NEJM 2024): 300 patients — overall UW-mRS 0.458 vs 0.374 (P=0.038); lobar subgroup: 0.517 vs 0.351 (P=0.010); anterior BG subgroup: non-significant
explanation.pearl: ENRICH is the FIRST positive MIS-ICH trial. Key points: parafascicular tubular retractor surgery, lobar ICH benefits (not basal ganglia), primary outcome is utility-weighted mRS (not dichotomised mRS).

questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with a 30 mL left putaminal ICH and IVH with obstructive hydrocephalus (GCS 10) has an EVD placed. Despite EVD function, IVH persists at 48 hours with bilateral ventricular casting. The CLEAR III trial is relevant to this scenario. What was the primary finding of CLEAR III?

options.A: Intraventricular alteplase significantly reduced death or major disability at 180 days (mRS ≤3: 48% vs 45%, P=0.001)
options.B: Intraventricular alteplase significantly reduced 30-day mortality (18% vs 29%, P=0.002) but did NOT improve functional outcome (mRS ≤3: 48% vs 45%, P=0.30)
options.C: Intraventricular alteplase significantly reduced both mortality and functional disability with no safety concerns
options.D: Intraventricular alteplase was associated with excess symptomatic haemorrhage, leading to trial termination

explanation.correct: CLEAR III (Hanley, Lancet 2017) randomised 500 patients with EVD-treated IVH to intraventricular alteplase 1 mg/8h (maximum 12 doses) versus saline placebo. Primary outcome (mRS ≤3 at 180 days): 48% vs 45%, RR 1.06, P=0.30 — NOT significant. However, 30-day mortality was significantly lower in the alteplase group: 18% vs 29%, RR 0.60, P=0.002. The mortality reduction without functional improvement reflects differential care withdrawal — more placebo patients had comfort care orders, suggesting survivors in the alteplase group were more likely to have severe disability (mRS 4–5).

wrong.A: The primary outcome in CLEAR III was NOT significant (P=0.30). The mortality reduction (P=0.002) was a significant secondary endpoint only
wrong.C: Functional outcomes were NOT significantly improved. The discordance between mortality and function reflects the self-fulfilling prophecy of early care withdrawal in the placebo group
wrong.D: CLEAR III was not stopped early for harm. The symptomatic haemorrhage rate was 2.2% in the alteplase group versus 0.4% in placebo — acceptable and trial completed full enrolment

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Intraventricular alteplase via EVD may be considered for IVH management (Class IIb)
explanation.trial: CLEAR III (Hanley Lancet 2017): 500 patients — mRS ≤3 at 180 days: 48% vs 45%, P=0.30 (negative); 30-day mortality: 18% vs 29%, P=0.002 (significant)
explanation.pearl: CLEAR III: mortality benefit (RR 0.60, P=0.002) but no functional benefit (P=0.30). This discordance reflects care withdrawal bias — survivors in the alteplase group had more severe disability. A paradigm example of mortality-function discordance in neurosurgical trials.

questions.stem: A 69-year-old hypertensive man presents with sudden onset left-sided weakness and headache. CT shows a 22 mL right putaminal ICH. GCS is 15. Blood pressure is 195/108 mmHg on admission. Using the INTERACT2 target, what should the blood pressure target be within the first hour?

explanation.correct: INTERACT2 randomised patients with SBP 150–220 mmHg to intensive treatment (target SBP <140 mmHg within 1 hour) versus guideline-based treatment (target SBP <180 mmHg). AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines recommend targeting SBP 130–150 mmHg for patients presenting within 2 hours of onset with SBP 150–220 mmHg (Class IIa). The INTERACT2 protocol specifically aimed for SBP <140 mmHg, which is the best answer here reflecting the trial protocol and current guideline recommendation.

wrong.A: SBP <160 mmHg is not the specific INTERACT2 target and falls between the intensive (<140 mmHg) and guideline (<180 mmHg) targets — not a validated treatment protocol
wrong.C: SBP <180 mmHg was the CONTROL arm target in INTERACT2 — the guideline-based management arm, not the intervention tested for benefit
wrong.D: MAP <110 mmHg is not the standard target used in ICH trials. Systolic BP targets are used in INTERACT2 and ATACH-II, not MAP targets, because SBP measurement is more standardised in clinical practice

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Class IIa: For patients presenting with SBP 150–220 mmHg within 2 hours, targeting SBP 130–150 mmHg is reasonable
explanation.trial: INTERACT2 (Anderson NEJM 2013): intensive arm target SBP <140 mmHg — safe, with trend toward benefit on ordinal mRS analysis (P=0.04)
explanation.pearl: INTERACT2 intensive target: SBP <140 mmHg within 1 hour. ATACH-II used same target but achieved faster/lower reductions — negative result. Current guideline: SBP 130–150 mmHg. AVOID SBP <130 mmHg — risk of perihaematomal ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 77-year-old woman is admitted with a 20 mL right lobar ICH. Her imaging shows multiple prior cortical microbleeds on SWI, predominantly affecting parietal and occipital cortices. She takes no anticoagulants. Her family asks about recurrence risk and whether her prior warfarin (discontinued 2 years ago for CAA risk) should be restarted for her AF. How should you counsel the family?

options.A: Annual recurrence risk for CAA-related ICH is 2–3%; anticoagulation restart is generally safe at 4 weeks
options.B: Annual recurrence risk for CAA-related ICH is 7–9%; anticoagulation restart is controversial and generally deferred ≥6–8 weeks, weighing thromboembolic vs rebleed risk
options.C: Annual recurrence risk for CAA-related ICH is 15–20%; anticoagulation should never be restarted
options.D: Recurrence risk is similar to hypertensive ICH (2–3%); anticoagulation restart depends solely on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score

explanation.correct: CAA-related lobar ICH has an annual recurrence risk of 7–9% (compared to 2–3% for hypertensive deep ICH). The distinction between lobar CAA-ICH and deep hypertensive ICH matters: CAA-ICH has higher recurrence risk, particularly with multiple microbleeds, APOE ε4 genotype, and prior ICH events. Anticoagulation restart after OAC-ICH for AF is controversial but not absolutely contraindicated. Current evidence (APACHE-AF, PRESTIGE-AF, RESTART observational data) suggests consideration at 6–8 weeks, particularly for mechanical heart valves (highest thromboembolism risk) or high CHA₂DS₂-VASc score, balancing the individual's estimated annual thromboembolic risk against 7–9% recurrence risk.

wrong.A: Annual recurrence of 2–3% applies to hypertensive ICH, not CAA-ICH. Anticoagulation restart at 4 weeks is too early — most guidelines recommend waiting at least 6–8 weeks and individualising risk
wrong.C: Recurrence risk of 15–20% is an overestimate. While some high-risk patients with multiple microbleeds may approach this, the average annual recurrence for CAA-ICH is 7–9%
wrong.D: Recurrence risk differs fundamentally between CAA-lobar ICH (7–9%) and hypertensive deep ICH (2–3%). CHA₂DS₂-VASc is relevant for AF management but does not capture the ICH recurrence risk, which must be weighed independently

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Annual ICH recurrence: hypertensive deep ICH ~2–3%; lobar CAA-ICH ~7–9%. OAC restart: individualise based on AF thromboembolic risk vs ICH recurrence risk; generally consider after 6–8 weeks
explanation.trial: RESTART (Al-Shahi Salman, Lancet 2019): antiplatelet vs aspirin restart after ICH — no excess recurrence at 2 years. APACHE-AF, PRESTIGE-AF: ongoing/recent RCTs for OAC restart post-ICH
explanation.pearl: CAA-ICH annual recurrence: 7–9% (vs 2–3% for hypertensive ICH). OAC restart timing: minimum 6–8 weeks, high individual risk stratification required. Never restart within 4 weeks.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with a 48 mL left lobar ICH and GCS 12. He is eligible for the ENRICH trial protocol (parafascicular MIS surgery within 24 hours). Compared to MISTIE III, what is the key difference in the ENRICH surgical technique?

options.A: ENRICH uses stereotactic frame-based catheter placement followed by intracavitary alteplase instillation
options.B: ENRICH uses a frameless neuronavigated tubular retractor (parafascicular approach) allowing direct surgical haematoma evacuation
options.C: ENRICH uses endoscopic intraventricular thrombolysis followed by external ventricular drainage
options.D: ENRICH uses transcranial focused ultrasound to liquefy the haematoma before aspiration

explanation.correct: ENRICH used parafascicular surgery — a transcortical approach using a tubular retractor (the MIPS system by Minovia Therapeutics) guided by neuronavigation. This creates a working channel through white matter fascicles (hence 'parafascicular' — between fibre bundles, causing less damage than broad retraction) to reach the haematoma cavity for direct mechanical evacuation. This is distinct from MISTIE III, which used stereotactic catheter placement followed by repeated intracavitary alteplase 1 mg/8h to gradually lyse and drain the clot chemically.

wrong.A: This describes the MISTIE III technique (stereotactic catheter + intracavitary alteplase), not ENRICH
wrong.C: Endoscopic IVH thrombolysis is a different technique (being studied in the INVEST trial) — not the ENRICH approach
wrong.D: Transcranial focused ultrasound (FUS) for ICH is an experimental technique at early feasibility stage — not the technique tested in ENRICH

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 (with ENRICH data) — Parafascicular MIS surgery for lobar ICH: first positive RCT evidence
explanation.trial: ENRICH (Ironside NEJM 2024): parafascicular tubular retractor (MIPS system); lobar ICH ≥30 mL; UW-mRS 0.458 vs 0.374 (P=0.038); first positive MIS-ICH trial
explanation.pearl: MISTIE III = stereotactic catheter + alteplase (chemical lysis). ENRICH = tubular retractor parafascicular (mechanical evacuation). ENRICH is the first POSITIVE MIS-ICH RCT; MISTIE III was negative.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man on enoxaparin 80 mg subcutaneously twice daily (therapeutic dosing for DVT) develops a 30 mL left parietal lobar ICH 8 hours after his last enoxaparin dose. Serum creatinine is normal. What is the most appropriate reversal strategy?

options.A: Protamine sulfate 1 mg per 1 mg of enoxaparin administered, achieving approximately 60% anti-Xa reversal
options.B: 4-factor PCC 50 IU/kg IV for complete enoxaparin reversal
options.C: Fresh frozen plasma to restore anti-Xa-inhibited factors
options.D: No reversal is needed as enoxaparin has a short half-life of 4 hours

explanation.correct: Low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) reversal with protamine sulfate is only partially effective. Protamine 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin achieves approximately 60% anti-Xa reversal (compared to >90% reversal of unfractionated heparin). The partial reversal reflects the fact that the shorter LMWH chains responsible for anti-Xa activity bind protamine less effectively than longer heparin chains. With the last dose 8 hours ago and a half-life of enoxaparin approximately 4.5 hours in normal renal function, residual anti-Xa activity is still significant and reversal is warranted. Maximum protamine dose is 50 mg regardless of enoxaparin dose.

wrong.A: This is the correct answer — protamine 1 mg/1 mg enoxaparin with ~60% reversal
wrong.B: 4F-PCC is not the standard treatment for LMWH reversal (unlike VKA reversal). There is no regulatory approval for LMWH reversal with PCC
wrong.C: FFP contains coagulation factors but does not directly inhibit or reverse LMWH anti-Xa activity. It would not significantly reduce anti-Xa levels
wrong.D: Enoxaparin therapeutic dosing half-life is approximately 4.5–7 hours. At 8 hours with normal renal function, there is still significant residual anti-Xa activity — reversal is warranted and important in the context of active ICH

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — LMWH reversal: protamine 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (max 50 mg); achieves ~60% anti-Xa reversal; dose reduce by 50% if >8h since last LMWH dose
explanation.trial: AHA/ASA 2022 Expert Consensus; Crowther MA et al. (NEJM 2002) enoxaparin reversal pharmacology
explanation.pearl: LMWH reversal with protamine is PARTIAL (~60% anti-Xa reversal). UFH reversal is COMPLETE (~>90%). Dose: 1 mg protamine per 1 mg enoxaparin. Maximum 50 mg. Reduce dose 50% if >8 hours since last LMWH dose.

questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with a 35 mL right putaminal ICH and GCS 11 is admitted to the neuro-ICU. Her family requests that a do-not-resuscitate (DNR) order be placed immediately, given her age and perceived poor prognosis. The ICH score is 2 (estimated 26% 30-day mortality). What is the evidence-based recommendation regarding early DNR orders in ICH?

options.A: DNR should be placed within 24 hours based on ICH score ≥2 to avoid futile resuscitation
options.B: Early DNR orders (within 24 hours) should be AVOIDED unless there is a clear advance directive or obvious futility, as they independently worsen outcomes through self-fulfilling prophecy
options.C: Family preferences override evidence-based recommendations; DNR should be placed immediately if family requests
options.D: DNR is appropriate for ICH score ≥3 but not for score 2; this patient should receive full resuscitation

explanation.correct: Multiple studies (Hemphill 2004, Zahuranec 2007, Creutzfeldt 2012) demonstrate that early DNR orders after ICH are independently associated with worse outcomes after controlling for severity measures, consistent with a self-fulfilling prophecy: early withdrawal of aggressive care prevents potential recovery. AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines explicitly recommend against early care withdrawal (within 24 hours) as the sole method of prognostication. Even an ICH score of 2 (26% 30-day mortality) implies 74% survival — aggressive care is appropriate in the absence of clear advance directives. Family preferences should be considered but must be balanced against medical evidence.

wrong.A: ICH score ≥2 is NOT a threshold for early DNR. The ICH score is a prognostic tool, not a decision tool for care withdrawal. ICH score 2 = 74% survival probability
wrong.C: While family preferences are important and must be respected, they should be informed by accurate prognostic information. Placing DNR purely on family request without medical justification in a patient with 74% survival probability would be ethically and clinically inappropriate
wrong.D: The evidence does not support a specific ICH score threshold for early DNR. The fundamental principle is avoiding early care withdrawal as the primary prognostic strategy

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Early aggressive care is reasonable and patients should receive full medical treatment for at least the first 24–48 hours unless a clear advance directive to the contrary exists
explanation.trial: Hemphill JC (Neurology 2004): early DNR orders independently predicted poor outcome in ICH after adjusting for baseline severity. Zahuranec DB (Neurology 2007): care withdrawal occurred in 21% of ICH patients, with significant practice variation
explanation.pearl: Early DNR in ICH = self-fulfilling prophecy. AHA/ASA 2022: do NOT withdraw care in the first 24 hours unless clear advance directive or obvious futility (bilateral fixed pupils, absent brainstem reflexes). ICH score 2 = 26% 30-day mortality = 74% SURVIVAL.

questions.stem: On non-contrast CT, a 71-year-old patient with a 40 mL left lobar ICH demonstrates a rounded, well-demarcated hypodense region within the main hyperattenuating haematoma, giving a 'black hole appearance.' This sign is surrounded by hyperdense haematoma. What is the name of this CT finding and what does it predict?

options.A: Satellite sign — predicts multiple haematoma foci and poor outcome
options.B: Blend sign — predicts haematoma expansion with high specificity
options.C: Black hole sign — predicts haematoma expansion (sensitivity ~33%, specificity ~97%) on non-contrast CT
options.D: Island sign — predicts anticoagulant-related bleeding origin

explanation.correct: The black hole sign describes a rounded or oval hypodense area surrounded by hyperattenuating haematoma on non-contrast CT. It reflects heterogeneous haematoma density due to active bleeding into already-clotted blood. It predicts haematoma expansion with sensitivity of approximately 33% and specificity of approximately 97% — highly specific but not sensitive. It is a non-contrast CT alternative to the CTA spot sign for identifying patients at high risk of expansion.

wrong.A: The satellite sign describes small separate haematoma foci adjacent to the main ICH — a different sign predicting expansion AND poor 90-day outcome. It does not describe a hypodense region within the main haematoma
wrong.B: The blend sign describes heterogeneous mixed density (hypodense mixed with hyperdense) on NCCT with a layering appearance — different morphology from the black hole sign's discrete rounded/oval hypodensity within hyperdense haematoma. Blend sign specificity is ~96%
wrong.D: The island sign describes ≥3 scattered small haematoma attenuations separated from the main haematoma margin — associated with anticoagulant-related ICH and predicts expansion with high specificity

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Non-contrast CT markers of haematoma expansion as prognostic tools; no specific guideline recommendation on management based solely on imaging sign
explanation.trial: Li Q et al. (Neurology 2015): black hole sign validation — sensitivity 33%, specificity 97% for haematoma expansion ≥33% or ≥6 mL. Li Q et al. (Stroke 2016): blend sign — sensitivity 36%, specificity 96%
explanation.pearl: Three NCCT expansion signs: BLEND sign (mixed density layering), BLACK HOLE sign (hypodense oval within hyperattenuation), SATELLITE sign (separate foci). All have high specificity (>90%) but low-moderate sensitivity. CTA spot sign (sensitivity 51%, specificity 85%) requires contrast but is the most studied.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man has a thalamic ICH of 12 mL with extension into the third ventricle causing obstructive hydrocephalus. GCS is 9. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate surgical management?

options.A: Stereotactic aspiration of the thalamic haematoma plus EVD
options.B: Open craniotomy and thalamic haematoma evacuation
options.C: EVD placement alone without haematoma evacuation
options.D: Bilateral EVD placement due to third ventricular obstruction

explanation.correct: Thalamic ICH presents a unique surgical dilemma. The thalamus is deeply located in eloquent brain, and direct haematoma evacuation (craniotomy or stereotactic aspiration) carries significant risks of neurological deficit (bilateral limbic/sensorimotor thalamic nuclei). For thalamic ICH with isolated hydrocephalus and GCS depression attributable primarily to hydrocephalus rather than haematoma mass effect, EVD placement alone is the appropriate intervention. This decompresses the ventricular system, treats the obstructive hydrocephalus, and allows monitoring of ICP without risking surgical injury to thalamic structures. AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines recommend against direct thalamic haematoma evacuation in most circumstances (Class III — harm).

wrong.A: Stereotactic aspiration of thalamic ICH risks significant morbidity from damage to thalamic nuclei, internal capsule, and nearby structures. The evidence does not support routine aspiration of thalamic ICH, particularly at 12 mL without severe mass effect
wrong.B: Open craniotomy for thalamic ICH has higher morbidity than stereotactic approaches due to extensive tissue disruption and is essentially never performed for primary thalamic haematomas
wrong.D: Bilateral EVDs are not routinely required for third ventricular obstruction — a single EVD placed in one lateral ventricle (typically right-sided) can adequately decompress both sides via the foramen of Monro

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Thalamic ICH: EVD for hydrocephalus (Class I); direct thalamic haematoma evacuation: Class III (harm) for most patients
explanation.trial: Observational data (Ziai 2018): EVD for thalamic ICH with hydrocephalus reduces ICP-related mortality without the risks of direct haematoma evacuation; no RCT for thalamic ICH surgery
explanation.pearl: Thalamic ICH + hydrocephalus → EVD only (NOT haematoma evacuation). The thalamus is eloquent and deep — direct evacuation risks bilateral sensorimotor and cognitive deficits. EVD treats the hydrocephalus component safely.

questions.stem: A 73-year-old man presents with a 22 mL right cerebellar ICH. GCS is 14, he is ambulatory with slight ataxia, and CT shows mild fourth ventricle effacement without obstructive hydrocephalus. Blood pressure is 180/100 mmHg. He has no prior history of anticoagulation. What is the MOST appropriate initial management?

options.A: Immediate suboccipital craniectomy due to cerebellar location
options.B: Prophylactic EVD placement to prevent future hydrocephalus
options.C: Medical management with blood pressure control and close neurological monitoring
options.D: Immediate transfer to interventional radiology for stereotactic aspiration

explanation.correct: The surgical threshold for cerebellar ICH is ≥3 cm (≈14 mL assuming a sphere) or the presence of brainstem compression, obstructive hydrocephalus, or neurological deterioration. This patient has a 22 mL haematoma (approximately 3.5 cm diameter), but is GCS 14 and ambulatory — key factors that modify the decision. He has mild fourth ventricle effacement but NO obstructive hydrocephalus (no dilated lateral ventricles). AHA/ASA 2022 guidelines support surgery for cerebellar ICH ≥3 cm WITH clinical deterioration OR brainstem compression OR hydrocephalus. In an alert, ambulatory patient without hydrocephalus, initial medical management with close monitoring is appropriate, with a low threshold for surgery if deterioration occurs. A 22 mL cerebellar ICH without hydrocephalus or brainstem compromise in an ambulatory patient is NOT an automatic surgical indication.

wrong.A: Immediate surgery is NOT indicated for any cerebellar ICH regardless of size if the patient is neurologically intact and there is no hydrocephalus or brainstem compression. The combination of clinical status, presence of hydrocephalus, and brainstem compression determines the surgical threshold
wrong.B: Prophylactic EVD placement is not recommended — it carries procedural risk and the risk of upward herniation if posterior fossa haematoma subsequently expands
wrong.D: Stereotactic aspiration is not the standard approach for cerebellar ICH — suboccipital craniectomy with open haematoma evacuation is the surgical technique of choice when surgery is indicated

explanation.guideline: AHA/ASA ICH Guidelines 2022 — Class I: Surgery for cerebellar ICH with clinical deterioration OR haematoma ≥3 cm with brainstem compression OR hydrocephalus. Clinical status (neurological examination) is the key modifier
explanation.trial: Kirollos RW et al. (Neurosurgery 2001): observational data on cerebellar ICH management — ambulatory patients with haematoma <3 cm and no hydrocephalus can be safely managed conservatively; surgical threshold is neurological status + CT features combined
explanation.pearl: Cerebellar ICH surgical trigger = volume ≥3 cm/14 mL PLUS brainstem compression OR hydrocephalus OR neurological deterioration. An ambulatory GCS 14 patient with 22 mL cerebellar ICH but NO hydrocephalus can be monitored — surgery is not automatic.
